items.foco: Risk factors: myopia, trauma, cataract surgery
items.title: Risk factors for retinal detachment
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors

  • Myopia >6 diopters: relative risk 3-6x due to axial elongation and peripheral retinal thinning.
  • Blunt trauma: produces dialysis tear in the ora serrata area (young) or tear in penetrating wound.
  • Surgery cataract: risk 0.5-1.6% post-extraction; major with YAG capsulotomy and intraoperative capsular rupture.
  • Family history of rhegmatogenous detachment increases the risk 3-5x.

items.title: Classic symptomatic triad
items.contentHtml:
Classic triad

  • Myodesopsias (floaters): sudden onset or sudden increase, due to remains of blood/inflammatory cells in the vitreous.
  • Photopsias: light flashes in the temporal visual field, due to vitreous traction on neurosensory retina.
  • Scotoma: progressive field defect from periphery to center; if it reaches the macula (macula off) low visual acuity.
  • ENARM question: in any patient with new photopsias and myodesopsias, rule out retinal tear with dilated fundus.

items.foco: Neurosensory retina detaches from the pigment epithelium
items.title: Pathophysiology of detachment
items.contentHtml:
Pathophysiology

  • The neurosensory retina separates from the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) by accumulation of subretinal fluid.
  • In rhegmatogenous detachment, liquefied vitreous fluid passes through a retinal tear and accumulates between both layers.
  • The RPE remains attached to the choroid; its ion transport and phagocytosis function is lost, causing photoreceptor degeneration.
  • The neurosensory retina is avascular in its outer layers; The detachment interrupts the supply of oxygen from the choroid, ischemia that progresses in hours.

items.title: Rhegmatogenous detachment
items.contentHtml:
Rhegmatogenous detachment

  • Definition: full thickness retinal tear that allows passage of vitreous fluid into the subretinal space.
  • Types of tear: horseshoe (focal vitreous traction), round (retinal atrophy), traumatic dialysis (ora serrata).
  • Predisposing factors: myopia, aphakia, honeycomb degeneration (lattice) with atrophic holes.
  • Emergency treatment: seal the tear with pneumatic retinopexy (expansive gas + laser/cryotherapy) or pars plana vitrectomy with endolaser and gas/silicone.

items.title: B-mode ultrasound in detachment
items.contentHtml:
B-mode ultrasound

  • Subretinal fluid is visualized as an anechoic space between the detached retina (echogenic V-shaped membrane) and the posterior wall.
  • Mobility: the detached retina shows undulating movement in dynamic examination (vs. fixed choroidal detachment).
  • If there is dense vitreous hemorrhage that prevents the fundus, B-mode ultrasound It is essential to diagnose rhegmatogenous detachment.
  • Fuchs sign: vitreous hemorrhage associated with retinal tear; On ultrasound, an echogenic point can be seen in the tear.

items.foco: Fuchs sign: vitreous hemorrhage in tear
items.title: Fuchs sign
items.contentHtml:
Fuchs sign

  • Fuchs sign: vitreous hemorrhage located at the base of the vitreous adjacent to a retinal tear.
  • Indicates that the tear is recent and has ruptured a superficial retinal vessel; Its presence increases the suspicion of rhegmatogenous detachment.
  • In ophthalmoscopy it is seen as a hemorrhagic ring around the tear or a blood veil in the lower vitreous.
  • Examination question: Fuchs' sign is associated with rhegmatogenous detachment and not with tractional or exudative detachment.

items.foco: Surgical urgency: pneumatic retinopexy or vitrectomy
items.title: Surgical emergency
items.contentHtml:
Surgical emergency

  • Rhegmatogenous detachment is a surgical emergency; Delay >1 week with macula off worsens visual prognosis.
  • Pneumatic retinopexy: indicated in single tears in the upper third, macula on, without proliferative vitreoretinopathy. Expansive gas (SF6 or C3F8) is injected and laser or cryotherapy is applied.
  • Vitrectomy pars plana: standard in multiple tears, macula off, vitreous hemorrhage or inferior detachment. It is combined with endolaser and tamponade with gas or silicone oil.
  • Postoperative: maintain postural position according to the location of the tear (face down if posterior).

items.title: Tractional detachment
items.contentHtml:
Tractional detachment

  • Main cause: proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR) with neovessels and fibrovascular membranes that contract and traction the retina.
  • There is no initial retinal tear; The detachment is concave, with smooth edges and without mobile subretinal fluid.
  • It can be complicated by iatrogenic or spontaneous (mixed) tear.
  • Treatment: vitrectomy with membrane dissection, panretinal endolaser and tamponade. Metabolic control prevents progression.

items.foco: Exudative detachment: choroidal inflammation or tumors
items.title: Exudative shedding
items.contentHtml:
Exudative detachment

  • Accumulation of subretinal fluid without tearing or traction, due to increased choroidal vascular permeability.
  • Causes: inflammation (posterior uveitis, serous choroiditis), choroidal tumors (melanoma, metastasis), malignant hypertension, Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome.
  • In the fundus: Detached retina with smooth and mobile surface, without tear. B-mode ultrasound shows homogeneous subretinal fluid and choroidal thickening if there is a tumor.
  • Treatment: etiological (corticosteroids in inflammation, radiotherapy in tumor). Does not require retinal surgery.

items.foco: Macula off vs on: visual prognosis
items.title: Macula off vs on
items.contentHtml:
Prognosis according to macular status

  • Macula on: the fovea is not detached; Preoperative visual acuity is preserved >20/40. Excellent visual prognosis if repaired soon.
  • Macula off: the fovea is detached; preoperative visual acuity <20/200. The prognosis depends on the time of detachment.
  • Post-surgical visual recovery is better if the duration of macula off is <1 week. After 1 week the photoreceptor degeneration is irreversible.
  • ENARM question: in macula off the visual prognosis is poor if the detachment lasts >1 week.

items.title: Poor prognosis
items.contentHtml:
Poor visual prognosis

  • Detached macula >1 week: final postoperative visual acuity rarely exceeds 20/200, even with successful anatomic reapplication.
  • Pathophysiology: apoptosis of photoreceptors due to ischemia and contact with subretinal fluid rich in cytokines inflammatory.
  • Other poor prognostic factors: advanced age, total detachment, grade C proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PRV), ocular hypotony.
  • RPV is the main cause of surgical failure; It manifests itself as epiretinal membranes that retract the retina.

items.title: Laser prevention
items.contentHtml:
Prevention of detachment

  • In symptomatic tears (with photopsias and myodesopsias) without clinical detachment, argon laser photocoagulation around the tear reduces the risk of progression to rhegmatogenous detachment.
  • Technique: apply 2-3 rows of confluent burns around the tear, creating a chorioretinal scar in 10-14 days.
  • In asymptomatic tears (incidental finding) without risk factors, the behavior is observation; the risk of detachment is low (<5%).
  • Question: laser is indicated in symptomatic tears to prevent rhegmatogenous detachment.
